spec_match.rationale: Uses getWeekInfo() / getCalendars() with fallbacks, then renders the current month using Intl.DateTimeFormat with the selected calendar instead of always showing Gregorian day numbers.

interactive.rationale: Locale dropdown drives a live month grid that re-rotates columns, shades weekend cells, and swaps day numbers/month labels for Islamic, Persian, and Hebrew calendar selections.

blurb_alignment.rationale: Index promises 'render a real month grid that rotates its columns and shades its weekend based on getWeekInfo(). Try Arabic, Hebrew, Persian locales.' Page delivers.

edge_cases.rationale: Renders non-Gregorian calendar selections now, but still silently falls back to `{ firstDay: 1, weekend: [6, 7], minimalDays: 1 }` when both method and legacy getter are missing.

references.rationale: See-also includes https://chromestatus.com/feature/5148228059398144 and the TC39 proposal.

design_system.rationale: Variables-only; weekend cells use --accent-rose or similar accent.

openQuestions.title: Show the underlying API call in a side panel
openQuestions.detail: A code block updated per-render showing `locale.getWeekInfo()` → `{ firstDay: 7, weekend: [5, 6], minimalDays: 1 }` would make the data-flow explicit and let visitors copy the snippet for their own apps.

openQuestions.title: Add a 'fallback used' indicator
openQuestions.detail: When neither getWeekInfo nor weekInfo is present the page silently uses defaults. Surface a small warning so users on browsers without the API know what they're seeing.

openQuestions.title: Cover the getTextInfo() direction property
openQuestions.detail: The grid should also flip RTL for Arabic/Hebrew/Persian locales — getTextInfo().direction would drive that. Currently the page rotates the week start but ignores text direction.

summary: Solid use-case demo for getWeekInfo() column rotation and getCalendars() calendar selection. The grid now renders Islamic, Persian, and Hebrew calendar months; a live API side panel, fallback indicator, and RTL direction handling would round it out.
